elements.section-2411-008: Relation Between Consecutive Occurrences and Closed Substrings
elements.sent-2411-437: In this section, we discuss a relation between consecutive occurrences of patterns and the notion of closed strings, which is proposed by Fici~\cite{Fici17 .}
elements.sent-2411-438: A string is closed if it is of length one or its longest border occurs exactly twice in the string.
elements.sent-2411-439: We observe that each consecutive occurrence of a pattern in a text corresponds to a closed substring of the text, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-440: Let $(i, j)$ be a consecutive occurrence of pattern $P$ in the text $T[1..n]$, then $T[i..j+|P|-1]$ is a closed substring with border $T[i..i+|P|-1]$.
elements.sent-2411-441: The maximum number of closed substrings in a length-$n$ string is $O(n^2)$ and this bound is tight~\cite{BadkobehFL15,ParshinaP24 .}
elements.sent-2411-442: Very recently, Jain and Mhaskar~\cite{Jain25 presented an $O(n \log n)$-time algorithm to compute a compact representation of all closed substrings of a string of length $n$ that uses only $O(n\log n)$ space, which is based on the notion of maximal closed substrings~\cite{Badkobeh0FP22 .}}
elements.sent-2411-443: In this section, we give an alternative, indirect representation of the closed substrings, which also occupies $O(n\log n)$ space and propose an algorithm to compute it.
elements.sent-2411-444: Lemma~\ref{obs:life-co is about the presence of a consecutive occurrence on the nodes of a heavy path.}
elements.sent-2411-445: More precisely, it states that in a heavy path, the nodes at which a consecutive occurrence is present forms of a subpath.
elements.sent-2411-446: The following lemma extends this result.
elements.sent-2411-447: The nodes in the suffix tree $\mathcal{T}$ at which a consecutive occurrence $(i, j)$ is present constitutes an ancestor-descendant path.
elements.sent-2411-448: Let $\pi_i$ be the path from the root to leaf $i$.
elements.sent-2411-449: Since all consecutive occurrences involving index $i$ are present only on the nodes of $\pi_i$, the node $w$ must lie on the path $\pi_i$.
elements.sent-2411-450: By the same argument used in the proof of Lemma~\ref{obs:life-co , it follows that the nodes of path $\pi_i$ at which consecutive occurrence $(i, j)$ is present form a subpath of $\pi_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-452: We denote by $\Pi_{i,j}$ the maximal ancestor-descendant path in the suffix tree $\mathcal{T}$ that corresponds to a consecutive occurrence $(i, j)$.
elements.sent-2411-453: Let $u$ and $v$ be the starting and ending nodes of $\Pi_{i,j}$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-454: Note that for each substring $P$ which is a prefix of $\str(v)$ and has $\str(u)$ as a prefix, the pair $(i, j)$ is a consecutive occurrence of that substring.
elements.sent-2411-455: Equivalently, $T[i..j+|P|-1]$ is a closed substring of the underlying string $T$.
elements.sent-2411-456: So, the collection of all such paths compactly encode all the closed substrings of the string $T$.
elements.sent-2411-457: The number of all such ancestor-descendant paths would be $O(n\log n)$, as the number of consecutive occurrences is bounded above by $O(n\log n)$.
elements.sent-2411-458: We present an algorithm that efficiently computes all these paths $\Pi_{i,j}$.
elements.sent-2411-459: The algorithm uses the method for computing sets $I_h$ of horizontal segments as a subroutine.
elements.sent-2411-460: The idea is to compute the set $I_h$ for each heavy path $h$, and suitably combine the paths (corresponding to the horizontal segments) to obtain the paths $\Pi_{i,j}$.
elements.sent-2411-462: Given the suffix tree $\mathcal{T}$ for a string, we can compute the path $\Pi_{i,j}$ for every consecutive occurrence $(i, j)$ present in the suffix tree $\mathcal{T}$ in $O(n\log n)$ time and space.
elements.sent-2411-463: We first compute the set $I_h$ for each heavy path in the decomposition, using our proposed algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-465: We then sort the segments in the set $\mathcal{I}$ by their associated consecutive occurrences using the radix sort algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-466: In the sorted list, the segments corresponding to a consecutive occurrence would be contiguous.
elements.sent-2411-467: On concatenating the subpaths (of heavy paths) corresponding to the segments of each consecutive occurrence $(i, j)$, we get the path $\Pi_{i,j}$ for the pair $(i, j)$ in the suffix tree $\mathcal{T}$.
elements.sent-2411-468: The algorithm for computing the set $\mathcal{I}$ of all horizontal segments takes $O(n\log n)$ time by Lemma~\ref{lem:preprocess-algo .}
elements.sent-2411-469: The radix sort for the set $\mathcal{I}$ would take $O(n + |\mathcal{I |)= O(n\log n)$ time.}
elements.sent-2411-470: The last step of concatenating subpaths for every consecutive occurrence can be done in overall $O(|\mathcal{I |) = O(n\log n)$ time.}
elements.sent-2411-471: Therefore, the total time is $O(n\log n)$.
elements.sent-2411-472: To summarize, we obtain the following:.
elements.sent-2411-473: The set of closed substrings of a string $T$ of length $n$ can be implicitly represented as $O(n \log n)$ paths over the suffix tree of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-474: Given string $T$, we can compute the representation in $O(n \log n)$ time.
elements.sent-2411-475: Note that the asymptotic complexities in Proposition~\ref{prop:closed are the same as those of the methods proposed in~\cite{Badkobeh0FP22 and~\cite{Jain25 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-476: However, our approach is based on textbook algorithms and admits a straightforward complexity analysis, whereas both existing methods require advanced dynamic data structures~\cite{BrownT79,KociumakaPRRW15 .}
elements.sent-2411-478: The structure can be built in $O(n\log^{5/2 n)$ time.}
elements.sent-2411-479: Further, we presented efficient algorithms for geometric problems involving weighted horizontal segments in a 2D plane, which appeared as subproblems in our approach and are of independent interest.
elements.sent-2411-480: Additionally, we noticed that the notions of consecutive occurrences and closed substrings are related.
elements.sent-2411-481: Using this relation, we provided an indirect compact representation of all closed substrings in a string, which, in a sense, is equivalent to the one recently proposed by Jain and Mhaskar~\cite{Jain25 .}
elements.sent-2411-482: We also presented an $O(n\log n)$ time algorithm to compute the representation.
elements.sent-2411-483: Another promising direction for future work is to explore alternative time-space trade-offs for the problems under consideration, with a focus on achieving optimal query time and/or (near) linear space.
